data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Strong Recent Growth In Oil Sales Volumes
data.description:
Dorchester Minerals (DMLP) reported a second consecutive quarter with a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes. This (along with lease bonus income) helped Dorchester increase its distribution by 10% in Q4 2025 despite relatively weak oil prices.

Since I last looked at Dorchester in December 2025, the oil price environment has changed considerably. At an average of $80 WTI oil in 2026 (and with a recent settlement), Dorchester's average quarterly distribution for 2026 may reach around $0.96 per unit. As well, Q2 2026's distribution should end up well over $1 per unit.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to several factors. These include strong near-term oil prices, an increase in modeled oil sales volumes, and an increase in my long-term WTI oil price from $70 to $75.

Q4 2025 Distribution and Sales Volumes

Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution increased by approximately 10% quarter-over-quarter to $0.755712 per common unit. This increase came despite softening oil prices during the last few months of 2025. Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution was also boosted by $6.0 million in lease bonus and other income, with over 50% of its full-year lease bonus and other income being recorded in Q4.

As well, Dorchester reported a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes (after a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in Q3 2025). Those increased volumes helped offset the impact of the 7% decline in Dorchester's realized oil price between Q3 2025 and Q4 2025.

Dorchester's Sales Volumes Q2 2024 Q3 2024 Q4 2024 Q1 2025 Q2 2025 Q3 2025 Q4 2025
Royalty natural gas sales (mmcf) 1,275 1,569 1,568 1,482 1,341 1,607 1,702
Royalty oil sales (mbbls) 423 642 535 518 399 493 592
NPI natural gas sales (mmcf) 493 642 510 436 493 581 453
NPI oil sales (mbbls) 131 198 140 136 163 160 162

Dorchester's Colorado acquisition (which closed in late August 2025) helped its Q4 oil sales volumes, but it also appears that there was significant quarter-over-quarter organic growth.

Recent Settlement

Dorchester announced that it had entered into a settlement with unrelated third parties to resolve litigation. This litigation involved Midland County leaseholds owned by Dorchester, and the settlement resulted in Dorchester receiving $15.5 million in proceeds that will be included in the calculation of the April 2026 Net Profits Interest payment.

This is a one-time boost that should increase Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution by approximately 31 to 32 cents per unit.

Outlook at $80 WTI Oil in 2026

I had previously modeled Dorchester's 2026 results based on 11,325 BOEPD (64% oil) in sales volumes. That was slightly above its Q3 2025 sales volumes.

Dorchester's Q4 2025 sales volumes jumped to around 12,100 BOEPD (68% oil), including a full quarter of contributions from its Colorado acquisition. Dorchester's sales volumes can vary significantly from quarter to quarter, but I am now modeling Dorchester's 2026 sales volumes at 11,750 BOEPD (66% oil).

At an average WTI oil price of $80, Dorchester may generate $204 million in 2026 revenues. Oil strip prices are quite volatile, but 2026 strip prices have ranged from the mid-$70s to mid-$80s recently.

For the purposes of this calculation, I've excluded the settlement mentioned above.

Revenue $ Million
Royalties $168
Net Profits Interest $24
Other (Including Lease Bonus Income) $12
Total Revenues $204

Dorchester would then be projected to generate $171 million in 2026 distributable cash flow.

$ Million
Operating Expenses $20
G&A $13
Total Cash Expenses $33

Thus, I now expect Dorchester to average around $0.88 to $0.89 per unit in quarterly distributions related to 2026. This excludes the $0.31 to $0.32 per unit in settlement payments, which would increase the quarterly average to around $0.96 per unit.

Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution seems likely to be fairly large. The Q2 cash receipts attributable to its royalty properties would mainly reflect oil sales from March 2026 to May 2026, a period where WTI oil may average $90+ (based on current strip). The Q2 2026 distribution would also benefit from the settlement payment, so it seems likely to be above $1.10 per unit and potentially approaching $1.30 per unit.

Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 per unit to $34.50 per unit. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

That $5.50 per unit increase is due to several factors, each of which is relatively similar in terms of the impact on estimated value.

One factor is the much higher 2026 oil prices compared to when I looked at Dorchester in December 2025, leading to strong near-term distribution expectations. In December 2025, WTI oil strip prices were in the mid-to-high $50s. As well, the settlement payment should boost Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution.

Another factor is my mid-single-digit upward revision in Dorchester's forward sales volumes, reflecting the impact of Dorchester's strong Q4 2025 sales volumes. I am still modeling Dorchester's forward sales volumes at several percent below Q4 2025 levels to be a bit conservative, though.

The final factor is a $5 increase (from $70 to $75) in my long-term oil price expectations, reflecting higher geopolitical risk going forward.

Conclusion

Dorchester Minerals announced a distribution above $0.75 per unit for Q4 2025, benefiting from strong oil sales volume growth and lease bonus income. Going forward, much improved oil prices may allow Dorchester's average quarterly distribution to reach close to $0.90 per unit during 2026, with a recent settlement adding over $0.30 per unit to its Q2 2026 distribution as well.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to a combination of strong oil sales volumes, excellent near-term oil prices, and a $5 per barrel increase in my long-term oil price.

data.title: Why Geopolitical Risk Is Fueling The Dollar's Surge
data.description:
By Erik Norland

With geopolitical risk in the forefront, many investors are asking a similar question: where should one turn for a safe haven? The Japanese yen, the Swiss franc, the U.S. dollar or gold? The reality is that there are no easy answers.

Since the Iran conflict began on February 28, the U.S. dollar has emerged as the primary flight-to-quality instrument, gaining around 3% while most foreign currencies fell against the greenback.

The Energy Advantage

In past crises, investors often fled to gold or the Japanese yen. Today, they're choosing the U.S. dollar – and energy is part of the reason why.

For decades, the yen was a reliable "flight to quality" instrument, but it has two disadvantages in the current environment. First, Japan produces very little of its own oil and natural gas.

Higher fuel prices mean higher import bills and slower growth, a dynamic that weighs directly on its currency. Second, Japan’s debt-to-GDP ratio stands at nearly twice that of the U.S. and six times that of Switzerland.

This debt burden didn’t matter when interest rates were stuck at zero. But now, with inflation above target and the Bank of Japan raising rates, long-term bond yields have surged. The soaring cost of financing that debt puts further downward pressure on the yen, altering the yen's role as a protective asset.

Conversely, the U.S. produces approximately 13.9 million barrels of crude oil daily, covering nearly all domestic demand. While energy-dependent regions like Europe, South Korea and Japan remain highly vulnerable to supply disruptions, America’s energy independence provides a distinct economic advantage. This asymmetry in energy exposure explains why the dollar is functioning as the preferred safe haven at the moment.

What About Gold?

Gold rallied nearly 100% in the year leading up to this conflict, meaning it entered the crisis already very expensive. Since then, it’s traded mostly sideways as many investors de-risk portfolios, selling equities and cryptocurrencies alongside precious metals.

Gold often anticipates inflation in advance. However, once that inflation actually arrives, the metal doesn’t always benefit immediately. Instead, rising inflation prompts central banks to raise interest rates or delay planned cuts, which makes fiat currencies relatively more attractive.

The Shift to Energy-Backed Currencies

With traditional safe havens showing mixed performance, where are investors turning? The focus seems to be shifting toward currencies of net energy exporters. For example, the Canadian dollar has strengthened against major currencies – Canada's oil production of approximately five million barrels per day, the vast majority destined for export markets, positions the loonie to benefit from elevated crude prices.

When it comes to an energy shortage crisis, which currency to select has become more important than ever.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Braze Remains Confident Of Revenue Growth Acceleration In The Coming Years, Maintain Buy
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Braze, Inc. (BRZE) previously with a buy rating, as I believed this stock should trade at a higher multiple given its growth potential and it being an AI beneficiary. For Q4 2026, bookings were very strong, enterprise traction continued well, and AI is now starting to show up in reported revenue. As such, I think BRZE deserves a higher valuation over time.

Q4 2026 earnings review

BRZE reported Q4 2026 revenue of $205.2 million, up 27.9% y/y from $160.4 million. Revenue included $5.7 million from BrazeAI Decisioning Studio, which implies 24.3% organic revenue growth for the quarter if we adjust for that. By segments, subscription revenue saw $193.5 million, while professional services and other revenue saw $11.7 million. Down the P&L, margins were lower y/y. Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 67.2%, down ~270 bps y/y, driven mainly by higher premium messaging volumes and hosting costs. That said, operating leverage was evident as non-GAAP EBIT grew to $14.5 million, almost double from $7.9 million last year, and non-GAAP EBIT margin expanded by 210 bps y/y to 7.1%.

Bookings and enterprise traction make the bull case stronger

There are plenty of data points from this past quarter that tell us growth is going to remain strong. I will start with bookings. Management said Q4 bookings were up a whopping >50% y/y and even said the average sales price reached a new high watermark with enterprise demand still especially strong. The pace of up-market penetration was also apparent as BRZE added 81 net new customers sequentially, and customers spending >$500k annually went up 35% y/y to 333. As of Q4 2026, those large customers now account for 64% of ARR, up 200 bps y/y. Also, BRZE ended Q4 with more than $1 billion of remaining performance obligations [RPO], up 30% y/y, with current RPO reaching $642 million, up 27% y/y. You put all these together, and the picture here is BRZE is not just growing; headline growth should see acceleration as the mix gets higher.

The other angle here is these large accounts appear to be using the platform in a much deeper way. Some data points cited by management include the following: among customers spending >$500k annually, >90% use Braze's SDK, >80% use Currents, and 50% already use Cloud Data Ingestion. While we don't know the revenue contribution, this is a strong testament to BRZE's upmarket push having substance. Customers are using the solutions, which makes BRZE even more embedded into how these customers collect data and run campaigns (i.e., it becomes harder to displace).

The AI aspect

Perhaps the biggest development to the equity story is AI impacts are starting to show up. For one, the BrazeAI Decisioning Studio contributed $5.7 million of revenue in the quarter. While that is small relative to the entire revenue base, it does provide real revenue evidence. With that, it provides the market with more confidence that BRZE's investments in AI solutions can ultimately be translated into revenue growth. This makes tracking the AI product roll-up/roadmap important, which has been progressing well. BRZE has launched both the Operator and Agent Console into general availability months ahead of schedule, and the early feedback was amazing. Operator was noted to already be used by more than two-thirds of customers after only a few weeks, while Agent Console adoption is growing on a week-on-week basis.

Importantly, management noted the operator is now accelerating and evolving workloads for thousands of BRZE dashboard users every week. This adoption momentum doesn't come from luck. It has to be the case that customers are finding value, and that means BRZE could monetize it eventually. If those are not enough to convince you BRZE is an AI beneficiary, consider that management said the AI roadmap helped with both win rates and deal velocity in Q4.

With that said, I would still caveat not to assume strong revenue contribution from AI in the near term, as management was clear that Agent Console revenue is recognized ratably over the life of the contract.

Valuation

The variant perception I have vs. the market is that I believe BRZE revenue growth will accelerate back to mid-20%, supported by growing enterprise penetration and more contribution from AI solutions. In my model, I expect BRZE to deliver ~$1.36 billion in revenues in FY2029, and the stock should be valued at a 6.5x forward revenue multiple if we compare it against its own trading history. Via this math, I think the stock price could roughly 3x from here, which is not really an impossible task since it once traded near $100. Note that the business is fundamentally stronger and margins are better today.

Risk

Q4 bookings and RPO were strong, but management also noted support from a large renewal quarter and a small increase in contract duration. This means that Q4 strength may not repeat, and if it slows down dramatically over multiple quarters, the market may not like it. Additionally, net retention is still below last year's levels. If renewals normalize and expansion stays muted, revenue growth could settle closer to 20% rather than reaccelerate.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on BRZE. The main reason is that the quarter strengthened my bull case that growth can move back toward the mid-20% range. Bookings, RPO, and large-customer growth all point to solid demand, while the AI roadmap has proven it can be translated into revenue. With that said, I would still watch the pace of near-term AI monetization and whether Q4 demand proves repeatable.

data.title: BGH: Reducing Duration Risk May Be Ideal For Today's Market Environment
data.description:
The Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with short-duration exposure to international high-yield debt issuances. With uncertainty growing in the US and European economies following the emergence of the Iranian war, I believe reducing duration risk may be prudent in order to optimize an investor’s fixed income exposure. While BGH can provide diversified exposure, investors may consider various ETFs as alternative investment strategies to capture similar high-yield exposure. Given the competitive costs and risks associated with the market environment, I am recommending BGH with a Hold rating.

Investment Thesis for Short-Duration High-Yield Fixed Income

The Iranian war has created substantial disruptions across the global economy, with energy prices exhibiting immediate effects that may ripple throughout other sectors in due time. Oil prices have stabilized above $100/bbl for both Brent crude and WTI and may remain elevated given the regional disruptions in the Middle East. According to the IEA, the Middle East provides roughly 30% of global oil and 17% of global natural gas production. With energy infrastructure and gas fields being critical targets of missile strikes resulting from the war, the global supply of oil and natural gas may not experience a recovery in the near future, resulting in higher prices for longer than expected.

The challenge is that there are no quick fixes to increasing production, let alone the transportation of commodities, relying on regions like Russia that had previously been embargoed due to the Ukrainian/Russian war.

While higher energy commodity prices are inflationary in nature, I believe the effects of higher energy prices have yet to trickle into inflationary data, potentially impacting other markets like consumer discretionary, transportation, petrochemicals, and other markets. Rationalizing this, higher gasoline and electricity prices may result in a reallocation of consumer spending and negatively impact consumer-facing markets. From a macroeconomic perspective, this could potentially result in higher inflationary pressures and weakening GDP growth.

This may place central banks in a peculiar position for 2026 as they weigh the impact of elevated pricing pressures on productivity. This could potentially result in a reversal of the rate-easing cycle experienced in recent years. If rates were to reverse course and begin climbing, long-duration bond strategies may suffer in price, whereas short-duration strategies may be relatively more insulated from changes in the key rate. This can be appealing for investors, as the pricing of the issuances held in the short-duration portfolio strategy may not be as significantly impacted by rate changes as those with longer-duration exposure. For reference, duration is the measure of price sensitivity relative to a change in the key rate.

As for the high-yield component of the portfolio, high-yield bonds typically trade directionally with the broader equity market, which may be the reason as to why BGH has faced a modest decline in share value in recent months. While rate policy may subtly influence the pricing of the portfolio, I believe broader market dynamics and economic expectations may be more heavily involved in the pricing of the strategy.

Specific to BGH, the fund’s top industries include health care & pharmaceuticals at 14.12%, energy at 10.39%, and telecommunications at 9.76%. The majority of bonds held in the portfolio are B-rated, making up 50% of the total assets. The strategy is primarily allocated to US-based issuances, making up 88.66% of assets. As of January 31, 2026, BGH was diversified across 174 individual issuances.

About Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund

BGH was launched by Barings on October 26, 2012, as a closed-end fund. The strategy has a management fee of 85 bps before applying leverage and other fees associated with the fund; the fund has a net expense ratio of 198bps.

BGH pays out a monthly distribution with a forward rate of $1.71/share, yielding 12.52%. Inclusive of the special dividend issued in August 2025, BGH paid out an annual dividend of $1.95/share over the last twelve months, yielding 14%.

BGH shares are currently trading at a -7% discount to NAV, its deepest discount since May 2025, presenting investors with an opportunity to purchase shares at a relatively appealing price.

As an alternative to BGH, investors that are more cost-sensitive may consider ETFs to gain high-yield exposure, including the iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG) and the Invesco Short Duration High Yield ETF (GTOH). For liquidity purposes, GTOH may be less ideal given that the fund only has $8.56 mm in net assets. While ETFs can provide high-yield, short-duration exposure, investors must weigh whether an active or passive management approach suits their investment needs. Given the rigid structure of GBH with respect to its allocation mandate, I believe ETFs can offer an appealing value. If the fund were to actively rotate duration in tandem with regional and sector exposure, I would argue that the managed strategy would be more ideal.

Risks Related to BGH

BGH is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with current income by allocating to short-duration, high-yield issuances, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making final investment decisions. High-yield bonds may exhibit greater credit risk to investors and may be significantly impacted by market dynamics as well as the risk of economic decline. High-yield bonds tend to trade directionally with the broader equity markets, potentially exposing investors to pricing risk if the equity market were to face further pressure. Geopolitical risks may influence the market, particularly as supply chains are disrupted as a result of the Iranian war. Investors will be exposed to manager risk, relying on the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight securities.

Final Thoughts

Short-duration, high-yield fixed income can be an appealing market to be in during a time of economic uncertainty. While the broader market may face certain headwinds resulting from the supply chain disruptions in the Middle East, reducing duration risk may insulate investors from central bank rate policy if they were to reverse course in 2026 if inflationary pressures rise. Given the competitive dynamics offered by ETF strategies and the rigid allocation strategy of BGH, I am recommending the strategy with a Hold rating.

data.title: Tsakos Energy Navigation: Performing Well In Strong Markets
data.description:
Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN) is a diverse, fast-growing, medium-sized oil tanker firm.

I have written about previously, most recently in late 2025. If you want to review my previous thoughts, you can see my last submission on them here:

Tsakos Energy Navigation: Upside Remains (NYSE:TEN) | Seeking Alpha

I wanted to write about Tsakos again as their share price has continued to perform very strongly. I wanted to check if the share price had gotten ahead of the fundamentals. And I can confirm that this is simply not the case. In spite of share price increases, Tsakos Energy Navigation remains a buy today.

Share price & introduction

Tsakos Energy Navigation is a diversified tanker company. They own a fleet today of around 62 ships; and they have a further 18 ships on order. In total they have around 80 ships. Over the next 2 - 3 years the company is growing its fleet strongly.

(Note: 1 VLCC is scheduled to be sold during Q2 2026)

Tsakos focusses on moving dirty and clean oil, and owns ships of all sizes. The below shows the share price in 2026 so far:

Clearly + 79% in 2026 so far is a great result! So I hope you owned shares! If you're wondering as to why the share price has done well, I think it's easiest to show a graph of Aframax rates lately, as they illustrate how tanker rates have been performing:

Look at the red line...rates for Aframax ships have increased almost 4x in 2026. For tanker companies, that increase in rates goes almost straight to their bottom line and is the reason share prices have increased of late.

At the time of writing, Tsakos shares are $40.19. There are 29.8 million shares issued, giving them a total market cap of just under $1.2 billion.

So why invest in Tsakos?

Nearly every tanker company has done well in Q1 2026. Geopolitical unrest - in particular the closure of the strait of Hormuz - has clearly been a massive driver of rates lately. The reason I like Tsakos in particular is that it has a reasonably significant level of debt on its balance sheet.

The current market cap is $1.2 billion and they have 62 ships on the water today. They have undertaken a huge newbuilding program. They have 18 additional ships due to hit the water of the next 2 / 3 years. Including these 18 newbuilds, they will have up to $4 billion in debt. When we see rates increase like we have in 2026 - you want to be exposed to companies with a significant amount of leverage. TEN fits the bill exactly - once debts are paid you - the shareholder - benefits fully from the increase in rates.

Tsakos' fleet

Tsakos does one thing that most of their competitor does not do. It publishes a data-kit that allows us to see how their fleet is performing on a regular basis. It was last updated in December 2025 and can be found here:

TEN_LTD_DATA_KIT_121825.pdf

This is a summary of their fleet.

Type Count
VLCC 3
Suezmax 20
Aframax 22
LR (Product) 4
Panamax (Product) 9
Handysize 2
LNG 2

Recently, they've agreed to sell a VLCC (for over $100m, delivered in mid-may) and have taken delivery of 2 MR vessels. So for Q1 they had 62 vessels on the water. Ultimately, they'll have a fleet of over 80 vessels in operation. For context, Nordic American Tankers has a very similar market capitalisation...but only has around 20 ships!

Q4 2025 and full year earnings

2025 was a strong year for companies involved in shipping oil; but as day-rates improved towards the end of the year, results improved in tandem.

For the full year 2025:

And for Q4 2025 specifically:

TEN earned $4.45 for the full-year 2025. TEN is trading at a share price of ~ $40 today. So based on their full-year earnings, TEN is trading around 10 x historical earnings. Personally I would suggest that's a reasonably fair price.

If we look at Q4 by itself and annualise that quarter alone, it's trading at just under 6 x earnings. Q4 was one of the better quarters the company has experienced. If the market conditions (i.e. the achieved day rates) for Q4 persist, then TEN is very cheap and is a buy today. Since the end of Q4, however, rates have continued to increase! The question now is whether or not those conditions will continue further into 2026 and the impact they have on the company.

Projecting Q1 2026 and beyond

Q4 2025 saw net earnings of $50.7 million. In the quarter the Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) earnings were $36,300 per day.

The below is a snip of their income statement for Q4 and for the full year of 2025:

The below is a snip of the most recent data-kit which TEN has produced. (Link is also provided above.)

You can see they take a relatively low-risk approach to chartering out their fleet. They tend to use a mix of:

1. fixed rates

2. rates with a floor and a profit share. (These are linked to indices)

3. some market or spot rates.

2) in particular is interesting, as I understand that the profit share is based on the index at the end of a month (or quarter)

The broker Poten & Partners lets us see current spot rates and also the average rates for 2026 so far.

We can now use all of the above to work out closely what TEN earned in Q1.

I have modelled their fixed, profit-share and spot market revenue using the 2026 average rates as provided above.

Items to note:

- I have given their VLCC on spot the benefit of the full quarter at $240k per day. This may prove slightly optimistic

- I have left the Maria Energy at $35.5k for the quarter

- I have ignored the addition of 2 new MRs as I don't know the rate they have signed up on.

Overall, I think their TCE for Q1 increased from $36,300 per day to $47,000 per day. Ultimately I think their net earnings will increase by + $50 million for the quarter to around $100 million. And their earnings per share will end up around $3.30 for the quarter.

With a share price of $40, TEN are able to add 8% of their market cap in just one quarter alone. And that is with most of their fleet still on fixed contracts! That seems exceptionally good value to me.

In terms of cashflows, I would estimate Q1 would provide $150 million (in net earnings plus depreciation - after interest repayments). That's equivalent to 12.5% of their total market cap, today.

Q2 2026 - an early estimate

Tanker rates in 2026 have been on a tear - there is absolutely no denying that. You can see it clearly in the Aframax graph I provided above. I used the average rates for 2026 in calculating a Q1 figure. But current spot rates for all shipping companies are significantly in excess of the averages for 2026 year to date.

We've started Q2 already so I think it's reasonable to try and project earnings for this quarter now. I'm going to use the spot rates provided above to work this out.

These are the spot rates I've used:

Type Spot
VLCC 376,800
Suez 215,000
Afra 345,400
Panamax 143,900
Afra LR 50,000

Notable items to point out for Q2:

VLCC

One vessel has been sold for around $100 million. I have removed it from earnings.

Aframax

3 are due to get renegotiated in early 2026. I have assumed the Sapporo Princess earns $50k per day for the quarter.

Panamax

And 8 of their 9 panamax are due to expire and get renegotiated in early 2026. For those expiring in Jan-March I am assuming they also earn $50k per day for the quarter

LNG

I have increased the Maria Energy to $80k per day. This is contracted at this rate, but I'm not exactly certain when this contract starts.

Using the above assumptions, I think that Q2 2026 could see a TCE of $67k per day. In terms of earnings, they would earn +$165 million in the quarter OVER Q4 2024. In total, they could earn around $215 million in the quarter, or over $7 per quarter.

Is that cheap? Based on the market today - I think they'll earn $10 per share in Q1 and Q2. So they'd earn 25% of the whole market cap in just 6 months. TEN remains incredible value here.

In terms of cashflows, TEN could take in over $400 million from Q1 + Q2 2026, or over 33% of the market cap. In a short 6-month period.

I acknowledge that these rates may not be sustained or sustainable - but they do highlight the torque that a company like TEN has today.

Balance Sheet

TEN is in a capital intensive industry. Every time it purchases an asset, they invest their own capital and take on debt. I have to point out that TEN has significant levels of debt on their balance sheet.

The below is their balance sheet as at December 2025:

They have liquid assets of just under $300 million. But they have debts of just under $2.1 billion, so they have a significant amount of leverage to deal with.

In addition they have a huge new-build program underway. The below outlines the newbuilds they have ordered, and the remaining payments due:

2026: $437 million

2027: $632 million

2028: $898 million

In total they have $1.967 billion in debt to take on, in addition to the $2.1 billion they owe today. That makes just under $4.1 billion total. Which is a very significant number indeed!

Are their debts affordable? Let's examine their cashflows

I think their debts are high, but I think they are definitely manageable.

TEN has $300 million in liquid assets now - which is a healthy cash balance.

In addition they have sold the VLCC Ulysses and will receive a price over $100 million. They should get the cash for this in Q2 2026.

In Q1 they should make profits of $100 million. Their depreciation per quarter is currently running at around $50 million. So in total I'd expect them to add $150 million in cash by the end of Q1.

In Q2, I think they could make up to $215 million. If we allow $50 million in depreciation expenses, they should bring in around $265 million by the end of Q2.

By the end of H1 2026, TEN could have:

Cash ($300m) + Q1 ($150m) + VLCC ($100m) +Q2 ($265m) = $815 million. Let's put this into perspective: that's 70% of their current market cap!

By the end of Q2 they could have sufficient cash to pay down 20% of their total debt of $4.1billion we mentioned above. Also we have to remember:

1. they are growing their fleet, so their earnings will grow in tandem

2. a large number of the newbuilds already have contracts attached to them. In particular the shuttle tankers have long, profitable, 15 year contracts attached

3. they will take on this additional debt but that debt will be added slowly over the next 2 / 3 years and not in 2026 alone

4. they will most likely sell older assets as time goes on, increasing their cash balance as they do so.

In summary: the $4 billion debt figure looks high, and I have tried to highlight it here. However it masks the fact that TEN's fleet is growing quickly and that current conditions will allow them to reduce their debt balance very quickly. The cashflows from excellent tanker rates, an expanding fleet + asset sales means they can repay these debts.

Risks to my thesis

I personally think that Tsakos is worth $50+ in the current environment. Every day that we see the Iran conflict continue, we see high demand for the ships that Tsakos owns. We see longer charters (e.g. from the US to Asia). And we see a world that craves energy security. All of this bodes well for owners of hard assets, like Tsakos.

That said - there are of course risks. Tsakos is a small cap Greek shipping company, so it's share price is volatile at the best of times. The primary risks that I see I have laid out here:

1. Global share prices and / or recession

Markets have been volatile for pretty much all of 2026. If there's a recession and / or if equity markets take a tumble, TEN will not be immune and will fall with it.

2. Tanker rates

Tsakos is reliant on continued demand for its ships. If that demand falls, tanker rates will fall, and ultimately Tsakos will suffer. As it has high debts, it could fall more than most. However we do have to remember that it has some proftection in the form of long-term contracts and a large number of fixed contracts as well.

Regardless if tanker rates fall, Tsakos's share price will drop as well.

3. Management

At the moment, Tsakos looks to be run well. They purchased ships - but they did so at a good time. They sold some older ships in tandem. And they've raised their dividend, but have done so prudently.

There's a risk that this changes. They could continue to invest in ships, irrespective of market conditions. This is a risk. That said, management is a large owner of shares in the company and is VERY aligned with common shareholders. I don't think it's likely they will prove to be bad actors and take on too much risk.

Summary...

Firstly I have to make clear that I cannot predict tanker rates for any period of time. Unfortunately I do not own a crystal ball. What I can say is that:

- tanker rates today are at very strong levels. All tankers are making money at these rates

- my best estimate for tanker rates for the near future (1 day / 1 week) is the current rates.

- over the medium term rates could & should normalise somewhat from here. But if they go down towards the levels of Q4 2025 over the next 3 months...owners of tanker firms should do very well.

In the case of Tsakos in particular, they had a very good Q4 2025. Since then, we can see that they have locked in a significantly better Q1 2026. And we're a few days into Q2 2026, we can see that Q2 is starting off exceptionally well too! Tsakos has a large number of ships on fixed rates, so it's somewhat protected if rates take a tumble as well.

Tsakos' share price has done well of late; you should be happy if you are + 80% in 2026 year to date! But I think that increase in the share price is completely justified. I think they will generate $10 in earnings by the end of Q2. Longer term, they have a large fleet, a strong backlog, are growing their fleet strongly and are the beneficiary of extremely high tanker rates today.

I have a price targe of $50 on them today, or +25%. I rate them a strong buy; ideally I would add on weakness.

data.title: Charter Communication: Value Trap Or Free Cash Flow Juggernaut
data.description:
Charter Communications (CHTR) is one of the largest telecommunications companies in the United States, serving over 32 million customers across 41 states. For decades, the company enjoyed a near monopoly for their extensive network of cable, fiber optic lines, and wireless infrastructure. However, over the past several years, the company has been squeezed on 2 fronts: 5G fixed wireless internet and growing fiber networks.

Verizon (VZ), AT&T (T), and T-Mobile (TMUS) have doubled down on 5G fixed wireless, which essentially is an inexpensive way to deliver basic home internet through 5G cellular technology. It requires no physical cable/fiber to the home and is simple to install. While 5G fixed wireless has slower speeds and is often far less reliable than cable/fiber, it serves a growing niche of customers who opt for the convenience and low price.

Squeezing the chart from the other end is the rapid build out of fiber by Verizon, AT&T, and Google (GOOGL). In many markets, Charter enjoyed years of little to no competition. This is no longer the case, with many customers being enticed away through promises of better performance and reliability through fiber offerings.

Market Punishment

Charter's slow bleed of internet customers has resulted in the market punishing the stock, which is down over 60% over the past 5 years. Some of this is warranted, as Charter has been forced to invest heavily to defend its market share against stout competition. Increased capital expenses and declining revenue are a sign of a company in trouble.

But by and large, Charter has already been punished by the market, which has brought the stock to dirt-cheap levels. The stock currently sports a P/E value around 6, which is well below its historical valuation. While this has been a bit of a nightmare for previous investors of the stock, prospective investors may want to kick the tires to see if the company has potential upside.

Investment Thesis

The market narrative surrounding Charter is that it is a dying company with its best days behind it. This is clearly seen in both the falling stock price and the dirt cheap valuation.

Year Annual Revenue Change vs. the previous year
2025 $54.8 billion -0.6%
2024 $55.1 billion +0.9%
2023 $54.6 billion +1.1%

However, revenue over the past 3 years is essentially flat. Charter is clearly not thriving in the current environment, but it's also not hemorrhaging revenue as many potential investors may have believed. In addition, net profit margin has hovered around 10% over the past 3 years, with a slight uptick in performance over the course of 2025. I'd argue the biggest red flag for a company is when it's forced to cut costs and reduce margins to prevent revenue decline. Clearly, Charter clears this hurdle with improving margins.

Where Charter really shines is its free cash flow generation. The company operates in a capex-heavy industry due to Charter's reliance on building physical cable/fiber through neighborhoods and evolving fiber upgrades. From 2023 to 2025, Charter averaged $11.4 billion in capex, giving the company a roughly 20% capex margin. Over those 3 years, it shows that despite revenue being essentially flat from 2023 to 2025, Charter has significantly grown its free cash flow.

Year non-GAAP Free Cash Flow* Change vs prior year FCF Margin
2025 $5.0 billion +16% 9.1%
2024 $4.3 billion +23% 7.8%
2023 $3.5 billion -43% 6.4%

*Non-GAAP FCF = Net Cash from Operating Activities − Capital Expenditures + Accrued Expenses Related to capital expenditures

Here is where the investment thesis for Charter takes off. Over the past several years, Charter has been heavily investing in rural line extensions and upgrading its existing network infrastructure to keep pace with its competitors' fiber buildout. The company expects 2026 to mark the end of the majority of the rural line extensions and mid-2027 to mark the end of its significant network evolution investment. As a result, the company expects its capex to dramatically decline over the next 2 years.

Source: Charter Communications presentation

This decline in capex is a major catalyst for the stock. From 2023 to 2025, under a steady capex cycle and flat revenue growth, the company grew its free cash flow margin from 6.4% in 2023 to 9.1% in 2025. Assuming flat revenue growth through 2028, the stock will grow free cash flow to roughly $9 billion in 2028. This is nearly a doubling of its free cash flow yield from 2025 to 2028. Below are some scenarios to view how a change in revenue could affect the free cash flow. In these scenarios, Charter would still have tremendous free cash flow growth even in a bear-case scenario where the company loses 2% of revenue every year until 2028. $8.0 billion in free cash flow would give the stock at its current valuation a free cash flow yield of 25%.

Year

Capex

Flat Revenue

non-GAAP FCF*

-1%/yr revenue

non-GAAP FCF

-2%/yr revenue

non-GAAP FCF
2025A $11.7B $5.0B -- --
2026E $11.4B $5.3B $5.2B $5.1B
2027E $9.5B $7.2B $6.9B $6.7B
2028E $7.75B $9.0B $8.5B $8.0B

*Non-GAAP FCF = Net Cash from Operating Activities − Capital Expenditures + Accrued Expenses Related to Capital Expenditures

These scenarios become even more interesting when investors understand management's plan for its free cash flow. The company has been aggressively buying back shares. In 2025, the company paid $5.4 billion to retire 20.1 million shares, or over 10% of outstanding shares. The trap many companies fall into and the reason share buybacks get a bad reputation is because companies seem to carelessly spend money on buybacks when times are good and the share price is high but abandon buybacks when the share price meaningfully declines. Charter's management has clearly stated their commitment to continuing share buybacks. At its current market cap of $31 billion, Charter will likely retire 16% of outstanding shares in 2026 alone, and this number will only increase as cash flow skyrockets in subsequent years.

As we've seen, even with Charter being squeezed by 5G fixed wireless and expanding fiber competition, Charter has kept revenue fairly steady and actually improved margins while at the peak of their capex cycle. The decline in capex starting in 2027 is an underappreciated catalyst in the stock.

The Path Forward

To prevent further customer losses and attempt to maintain revenues and margins, the company is trying to make its existing customers as sticky as possible. The internet business itself is inherently a fairly sticky model. Most customers stay with their existing internet because it can be a huge hassle to change providers. To make their customer base even stickier, the company has successfully started bundling internet, mobile, and video as a package. The early results of this bundling strategy are encouraging. While Charter's internet customer base contracted by approximately 1.3%, or roughly 400,000 subscribers, to 29.7 million in 2025. Mobile line additions rose 19%, or 1.9 million subscribers, to 11.9 million in 2025. As the mobile penetration of Charter's internet base continues to rise, management expects the churn benefits to become a powerful tailwind for internet stability, the profit engine of the company.

Another area of growth going forward is Charter's growing rural footprint. In 4Q25, Charter saw 46,000 net customer additions in their subsidized rural footprint. The company also grew its subsidized rural passings by 483,000 in 2025 and expects another 450,000 in 2026 as it winds down its last large build year. These additions are important, as many rural homes have extremely limited options for broadband, and Charter is seeing a decent conversion rate. While these new additions aren't massive, they do help stem losses from pressure Charter is seeing from 5G fixed wireless and fiber competitors.

Take Away

The negative narrative around Charter is overdone, and potential investors should take a fresh look at the stock. At current levels around $220, the stock looks attractive across several levels. From a valuation perspective, you're looking at one of the lowest P/E ratios in the entire S&P 500, at around 5-6. The company's ability to generate cash will only improve over the next several years. Its trailing 12-month free cash flow yield is a robust 16.1%. Perhaps most important is the company's upcoming free cash flow boom that will see free cash flow rise from around $5.2 billion in 2026 to $8.0 (bear case) - $9.0 (bull case) billion in 2028. This gives the company plenty of optionality to reward shareholders through aggressive share buybacks or chip away at their debt load.

data.title: FX Technical Levels: NZD/USD Tests Major 0.57 Support Ahead Of RBNZ Meeting
data.description:
By Elior Manier

NZD/USD has been thrashed since the start of the US-Iran war, amid heavy concerns about energy supply in the Land of the Kiwi. At the beginning of 2026, the New Zealand dollar was on a powerful run. Traders priced higher odds for rate hikes, while most other central banks were priced for pauses and cuts. Since then, pricing has dramatically shifted.

Numerous policymakers now hint at incoming rate hikes to diminish the inflationary impact of 60% rises in oil prices. Having lost some relative strength, the new Governor Anna Breman failed to forecast a significant hawkish turn that had been anticipated by market participants earlier in the year. You can access her recent speech right here.

Having missed its Q4 GDP data, released in mid-March, traders quickly began pricing out any signs of heating in the NZ economy. Combine these factors with a gigantic rise in the petrodollar since March, and traders got exactly what they needed to not only take profits on previous bullish views but also reverse them into a bearish trend – the major pair is down 4.75% since.

The upcoming meeting is strongly priced for a pause (about 90% odds). However, traders have priced in 60 basis points of hikes for the rest of the year.

Given the turn in fundamentals and upcoming event, the drops in NZD/USD might just be over. Traders will focus on New Zealand macro data and especially the Royal Bank's inflation outlook for the next meeting (May 27, 2026).

Combine high odds of more hawkish communications with daily bullish divergences (see below), and the NZD/USD could be poised for a decent upside reversal – the rest will be to see if it leads to a proper uptrend.

To prepare for the upcoming key RBNZ meeting this evening, it is appropriate to conduct a multi-timeframe analysis of NZD/USD and assess potential scenarios.

NZD/USD Multi-Timeframe Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

The Kiwi dollar has entered a significant downtrend since beginning March, easing from 0.60 right below 0.57. Subject to heavy profit taking from its mid-January gigantic rise, the selloff accelerated as concerns for antipodean crude delivery kept looming over the NZD buying power.

The conflict is nearing an important turning point; a de-escalation deadline is about to expire, so if the situation worsens, the outlook can be quite cloudy – To help yourself spot a direction, it can be quite essential to look at the US Dollar Index (DXY), remaining close to 100.00. Rejecting its highs will help NZD/USD to rebound.

If de-escalation occurs, NZD/USD is poised for a consequent reversal, particularly as the RBNZ is expected to tighten the screws for a potential future hike. Combine this with prices reaching the bottom of its main downward channel, and technicals are also corroborating this view.

Be careful, as monetary policy meetings can be quite unpredictable; hence, the largest traders will await the decision to move their pawns. Let's take a closer look to spot levels of interest for the ongoing correction.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

The immediate action is still indecisive, as seen with the RSI hanging around the neutral zone. To tilt the scales, however, the recent dip below 0.57 could not hold; hence, bulls could be getting the upper hand in coming times. This will need to be confirmed after the RBNZ meeting (tonight) and particularly after a break of the 4H 50-period MA (0.57275).

Trading Levels for NZD/USD:

Resistance Levels

  • 4H 50-period MA (0.57275)
  • 0.5770 to 0.5790 major momentum pivot (channel highs)
  • 0.5850 December high pivotal resistance
  • 0.5885 to 0.59 minor resistance
  • September 2025 pivot area 0.60 to 0.60150

Support Levels

  • Main support 0.57 (+/- 150 pips) – testing
  • War lows: 0.56825
  • Minor support: 0.5650
  • January 2025 support: 0.56

1H Chart

Looking even closer at the 1H timeframe, the major FX pair marks heavy indecision as traders await the key fundamental events to make their moves.

  • Above 0.57275, bulls take the upper hand, which would point to 0.58850 (channel highs).
  • Below 0.5690, bears retake control and point to further selling acceleration.

Safe trades, and good luck for the upcoming meeting!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rare Earths: Policy, Economic Resilience Drive New Magnet Supply Chains
data.description:
Rare earth elements have come under increasing focus owing to their criticality to defense equipment manufacturing and clean technology. China has monopolized this supply chain for over two decades and has not shied away from using it for geopolitical leverage, forcing others to think of a diversification strategy. This is the first article in a five-part series in which Platts News explores the strategies under play and the challenges they face.

Rare earth elements are now indispensable to modern technology, driving advancements across the energy transition, electrification, automation and defense sectors.

As global demand for these critical metals surges, their strategic importance — and the risks of supply concentration — have come into sharp focus. This overview explores the pivotal role of rare earth elements (REE) in manufacturing, their diverse applications, and the evolving landscape of supply chain resilience shaped by government policy and private investment.

REEs comprise 17 metals, including the 15 lanthanides plus scandium and yttrium. They are foundational to the world's most advanced and rapidly growing technologies, spanning energy, digitization and aerospace. In manufacturing, REEs are essential for high-strength permanent magnets, enabling compact, energy-efficient electric motors and generators. These components are critical to electric vehicles, industrial robotics and wind turbines.

Beyond manufacturing, REEs play a vital role in consumer electronics, such as smartphone vibration motors and display color phosphors. They are also integral to energy and environmental technologies, including catalysts, refining processes, and emerging clean-tech supply chains. Defense and aerospace systems rely on REE-based components for precision guidance, sensors, radar, and high-temperature alloys — making supply chain resilience a matter of national security.

Demand growth drivers

Magnetic rare earths — neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium and terbium — account for roughly 30% of total REE volumes but over 80% of market value, according to McKinsey.

Global demand for magnetic rare earths is expected to triple from 59,000 metric tons in 2022 to 186,000 mt by 2035, McKinsey forecast in a July 2025 report. This growth is driven primarily by automotive applications, projected to expand at a compound annual growth rate of about 10% between 2025 and 2035. Wind and machinery segments are expected to grow by 5% to 6%, while appliances will see more modest growth of around 3% over the decade.

McKinsey senior partner Michel Van Hoey told Platts, part of S&P Global Energy, that most of this demand centers on dysprosium and terbium, which enable magnets to operate at higher temperatures — a key requirement for hybrid and electric vehicles. Accordingly, McKinsey projects demand for dysprosium and terbium, or DyTb, to grow at a CAGR of about 8.8%, outpacing the 6.1% CAGR forecast for neodymium and praseodymium, or NdPr, which remains the dominant magnet material.

Supply disruptions have prompted some automotive original equipment manufacturers to explore alternative motor designs that use fewer or no REE. However, these alternatives often result in lower power density and increased reliance on other critical minerals such as copper.

Mine-to-magnet supply chain initiatives

McKinsey's findings reflect broader industry trends, with governments and OEMs actively seeking REE supply chains independent of China. China accounts for nearly 60% of global mining, over 85% of processing capacity, and more than 90% of permanent magnet production, according to the US Commerce Department. This makes REEs a strategic vulnerability for governments and industry alike.

In the US, policy has focused on permanent magnets and developing a secure "mine-to-magnet" supply chain, encompassing sourcing, separation, processing, metallization, alloying and magnet manufacturing. The US Defense Department has been especially proactive, investing in established REE and magnet projects through its Manufacturing Capability Expansion and Investment Program.

In July 2025, MP Materials Corp. (MP) announced a $400 million public-private partnership with the US Defense Department. The deal included a preferred stock investment and a 10-year agreement granting the department rights to 100% of the permanent magnet output from MP Materials' planned 10X facility.

Expected to begin commissioning in 2028, the plant will boost MP Materials' magnet capacity to around 10,000 mt per year, supplying defense applications such as drones, robotics and advanced semiconductor fabrication.

Australia's Lynas Rare Earths Ltd. (LYSCF)(LYSDY) has strengthened its US presence through a binding letter of intent with the Defense Department, covering about $96 million in light and heavy rare earth oxide purchases over four years. Lynas also remains a key supplier to Japanese permanent magnet producers and recently extended a long-term offtake agreement with Japan Australia Rare Earths BV, citing new Chinese restrictions on exports of "dual-use items," including REEs, to Japan.

Under the extension, Lynas will supply up to 7,200 mt/year of NdPr to Japan through 2038, and make 75% of its heavy rare earth oxide output available to Japanese customers.

In Brazil, Serra Verde Mineracao Ltda. secured $565 million in financing from the US International Development Finance Corp. to support operations at its Pela Ema deposit in Goiás, where commercial production began in early 2024.

With a product mix rich in dysprosium, terbium and other heavy rare earths, Serra Verde aims to expand output from 5,000 to 6,500 mt/year of total rare earth oxide by end-2027.

New REE and permanent magnet projects are also attracting strong interest from US government agencies. In October 2025, project developer Pensana PLC received a letter of interest from the US Export-Import Bank for up to $160 million in debt funding to support the development of its Longonjo REE mine in Angola.

Production at Longonjo is scheduled to commence in early 2027, with an initial output of 20,000 mt/year of clean mixed rare earth carbonate. A planned second phase could double production to 40,000 mt, representing about 5% of current global rare earth material production.

"OEMs are looking for supply chain resilience — today that means non-China. They want certainty about supply chain provenance at all links, and once they have realigned from China, they want to be able to scale," Paul Atherley, Pensana chairman, told Platts.

Atherley's observations were echoed by REalloys Inc. CEO Lipi Sternheim, who told Platts that demand for rare earths and permanent magnets sourced outside of China is accelerating, driven by government policies and supply-side constraints.

REalloys (ALOY) received a letter of interest from the US Ex-Im Bank in 2025 for a loan of up to $200 million to support the development of its integrated mine-to-magnet North American supply chain. The company's portfolio includes the Hoidas Lake REE property in Saskatchewan and downstream magnet manufacturing operations in Euclid, Ohio.

"Over the next decade, we expect sustained growth in offtake interest from OEMs, Tier 1 suppliers, and government-linked buyers seeking secure, traceable and geopolitically aligned supply chains," Sternheim said. "From our perspective, this demand is not cyclical — it is policy-driven and supply-constrained."

Platts has proposed four first-of-their-kind price assessments for REEs, focusing on mine-to-industry alternative supply chains. The new assessments launched on March 31.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Alphabet's Growth Story Is Being Misread
data.description:
Introduction

What makes Alphabet (GOOGL) an exciting investment prospect currently is the fact that it is one of the key players in the AI revolution but is undervalued relative to its peers amid all the buzz around them.

What I think explains the underperformance of the stock is that it is not due to any weakness in its finances, but merely that the market is concerned about the possibility of disruption within the Search category and rising capital expenditures related to AI.

AI Is Expanding the Economic Surface of Search

The latest earnings results of Alphabet did not come with any surprises; however, the report contained several trends that have changed the overall impression about the company's future prospects. The Q4 revenue increased by 18% year over year to $113.8 billion, and net income increased to 30%.

For some reason, there's a narrative that AI will hurt the search business. It's not right, at least in this situation. Instead of shrinking the business, AI is driving search engine evolution. According to management, users are interacting with it differently; they ask more complicated questions and conduct longer conversations that involve follow-up questions after initial ones. All this can be attributed to AI usage; thus, it's not cannibalization but rather increased engagement of users.

Moreover, these longer and more complicated questions were hard to monetize historically since it was hard to understand the exact intent of the user. Now AI can help with that and allow matching queries to ads, hence boosting monetization efficiency. In other words, AI can drive growth by increasing both the volume of queries and their monetization efficiency. It's hard not to like such a perspective.

Cloud Is No Longer Optional, It's Core

Search is evolving fast, and Cloud continues to drive the same trend. For instance, in Q4 its revenue rose by 48%, reaching almost $17.7 billion, and its backlog increased to $240 billion (+55%). Both these results are significant since they reflect high-level execution and demand for Cloud services. By the way, 75% of Cloud's customers are now using AI-based solutions, which, in turn, makes them adopt 1.8x more services, hence creating a positive platform effect.

Also, note that operating margins of Cloud business increased to around 30% from 14.5% yoy. Therefore, Cloud business is becoming equally important as Search since it generates higher margins, scales faster, and enjoys a huge backlog.

The Gemini Layer: Distribution at Unmatched Scale

Another important component of this story is Google's AI capabilities as it distributes them at an unmatched scale. The Gemini app got 750 million MAUs in just several months after launch. Also, management reported that since the introduction of Gemini 3, engagement intensity and user retention increased dramatically.

The same is true for enterprise usage of AI. Over 8 million paid Gemini Enterprise seats were sold in just four months of operation. As the technology becomes more advanced, users embed it into their processes, hence making the switching costs higher with time.

What's important is that such a distribution allows Alphabet to integrate AI into all its product lines like Search, Cloud, WorkSpaces, Chrome, Android, and others. This allows the company to offer users unmatched distribution compared to the competitors who compete by having models that are superior to those provided by Alphabet. So, this gives an advantage to Google since distribution matters more often than people expect.

Capital Intensity Is the Trade-Off

Of course, all this is not free. The CEO said that next year the company plans to spend $175-$185 billion on capex. Such an investment amount forces me to change the perception of this business since Alphabet is gradually becoming an infrastructure provider, which requires huge investment to generate revenues.

Management said that demand is currently supply-constrained. This means that if the company invests money in capacity today, it will generate returns in the future. In my view, it's risky, but at the same time it's the only right thing to do at this point in time.

Also, note that such large capital intensity will influence the company's profitability since depreciation expenses will increase. Nevertheless, the total FCF generation remains decent, with $73.3 billion in the full year. From this perspective, I would say that Alphabet faces new challenges and risks, which force me to rethink the stock valuation and overall attractiveness.

Valuation, Still Attractive, But Less Obvious

GOOGL appears to be expensive despite being cheaper than the peer group. For example, GOOGL stock trades for ~$300, which translates to 26x 2026 earnings and 22x 2027 earnings, considering the consensus EPS of $11.56 and $13.43. In other words, GOOGL enjoys a two-year EPS CAGR of ~16%, which appears conservative considering the 48% growth rate in Google Cloud and AI-related monetization.

However, the forward P/S ratio drops from 7.6x to 6.6x as revenues accelerate towards $540 billion. In addition, free cash flow is expected to exceed $70 billion per year, trading near low-20s P/FCF despite accelerating AI-driven growth, implying strong cash generation is still undervalued relative to future scalability and thus offering protection against the downside.

Yet, what investors miss out on is the operating leverage offered by AI. With monetization improving across search, cloud, and Gemini, margins can significantly improve against the backdrop of a fixed cost base. Therefore, although multiples are higher than traditional players, multiples appear cheap compared to growth potential and platform depth.

Risk Assessment

Alphabet’s investment is upwards of ~$185 billion in CapEx, becoming a capital-intensive company that will become a key provider of AI infrastructure. Everything about how they’re being funded changes. The question is whether such large-scale CapEx will yield monetary gains through monetization or just an increase in volume.

Any pressure on pricing, inefficiency in utilization, or even monetization of AI (Gemini/AI Search) would be worrisome. Because of that scale, any inefficiencies are amplified. Even getting the timing and economic considerations wrong may not necessarily mean disaster, but they will definitely limit their upside.

Conclusion

Alphabet is no longer simply a premium advertising company, but it is becoming an entire stack of AI technologies with various revenue layers. Wall Street continues to view it skeptically because of CapEx considerations, but it is precisely here that I find the investment case compelling. In essence, what I am looking at is a company generating earnings, growing its TAM, and becoming embedded within both individual and enterprise workflows.

data.title: Right Tail Capital Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
During spring break this year, our family spent several days in Kiawah Island, South Carolina. What struck me most about the place was how thoughtfully it blends the old and the new. Ancient live oaks and preserved marshland with tons of alligators sit alongside newer homes, parks, and family activities.

The most effective expressions on the island combine both. One afternoon, we walked through Night Heron Park, where a newly built deck winds around trees that have been standing for decades. The old and the new, woven together. The result is better than either would be alone.

Stock market commentary often feels like a frantic search for the new — the next technology, the next geopolitical conflict, the next industry shift, the next market narrative. Yet many of the principles that drive long-term investment success have remained remarkably stable. Jeff Bezos famously remarked that he is more interested in thinking about what will not change over the next decade than what will. That framing feels particularly helpful today.

At Right Tail, the foundation of our approach remains deep fundamental research, long-term ownership of high-quality businesses, and patience as compounding does its work.

Durable competitive advantages, high returns on incremental invested capital, aligned management teams, and reasonable purchase prices continue to matter enormously. Time remains one of the most powerful forces in compounding. I continue to study industries and businesses, both ones we own and ones we could potentially own.

While these principles remain constant, the tools available to investors are evolving rapidly.

Artificial intelligence is improving how information is gathered, processed, and synthesized. I have been spending more time with these tools thoughtfully — not to replace fundamental analysis, but to make the research process more efficient.

AI can help summarize large bodies of information, highlight inconsistencies across documents such as a management team’s changing narrative over several years, and surface questions worth investigating further. One example is I’ll often submit questions to one of the LLMs about a company while I’m researching it. In the past, I’d compile these questions into a list to work on later or discuss with the company. Now, AI can answer many questions almost instantaneously. Tasks that once took hours can sometimes be completed in minutes, which creates more time for the work that matters most: thinking carefully about businesses and long-term capital allocation.

However, AI is not a substitute for judgment. It does not meet management teams, assess incentives, understand the nuances of corporate culture, or develop conviction built over years of studying an industry. Those elements still require human experience, skepticism, and patience.

The benefit is not that AI makes investment decisions easier. Rather, it allows more time to focus on the highest-value activities — understanding businesses deeply, refining investment decisions, and communicating with investors.

The core philosophy of Right Tail remains unchanged. What is evolving are the tools that help support the work. In many ways, the role of technology in investing is similar to what we see in places like Kiawah. New tools can enhance the experience, but the underlying foundation remains the same.

Current Markets

Markets have been volatile as investors attempt to understand the implications of artificial intelligence across industries. Geopolitical tensions, including the conflict between the US and Iran, and other macro developments have also contributed to uncertainty.

I do not know how these events will unfold, and I'm pretty sure neither does anyone else. Rather than attempting to predict them, my focus remains on identifying businesses with durable economics and management teams capable of navigating uncertain environments.

Periods like this often create opportunities. Uncertainty can lead to price dislocations that allow patient investors to purchase strong businesses at attractive prices. I am digging into these pockets of uncertainty and always comparing them to our current portfolio holdings.

We have seen similar periods before.

The current market appears to reward two types of companies: those perceived to benefit directly from AI and those believed to be insulated from it. This atmosphere of technological fear reminds me of 2017, when Amazon was believed to be on the verge of disrupting every business model from industrial distribution to aftermarket auto parts. While Amazon did disrupt some, many fears proved overstated. Over time, the market rewarded the underlying cash flow-generating power of the survivors. I suspect we will look back on the current AI-driven volatility in a similar way.

We are balancing our core investing principles of deep fundamental research in a time when uncertainty seems high. Over time, the market will reward the long-term cash flow-generating power of businesses. We will continue to invest with a long-term mindset, selecting the companies most likely to continue to thrive.

RTC Portfolio Spotlight - NRP

NRP is predominantly a coal royalty company owning 13M acres of mineral interests. In any given year, free cash flow is typically 80%+ from mineral royalties and up to 20% from their JV in a soda ash mining company. Of the coal royalty revenue, 65-75% typically comes from metallurgical coal royalties, with the remainder from thermal coal royalties. I like that the business is primarily exposed to metallurgical coal, a key ingredient in making steel and one where limited new supply is being added.

Royalty companies earn revenue from any mining that is done on the land they own. They'll work out a contract with the mining company as to what percentage of revenue needs to be paid as a royalty. These can be fantastic businesses, as I learned during my time at T. Rowe as a small cap metals and mining analyst. I came to appreciate companies like Franco Nevada, a precious metals royalty company with an eye for the long term. With properly capitalized royalty companies, time is really your friend in a number of ways. Land ownership allows the royalty companies to ride out the more difficult times. Furthermore, royalty companies benefit from significant operating leverage when commodity volumes and prices increase, as their minimal costs (operating margins are typically 70-75%) remain unchanged. With a producer, it's not uncommon to see rising costs accompany rising prices. Based on my estimates, NRP has 25+ years of remaining reserves on its metallurgical coal royalties and 70+ years of remaining reserves on its thermal coal royalties. That's a lot of duration. And yet the business trades at a high single digit multiple of normalized earnings. The market is saying we can only capitalize 8-10 years of earnings, yet we have 25+ years of reserves.

So what is the disconnect and why does the opportunity exist? For starters, cash flows have declined as each of their commodities are in year 3 of a bear market. From 2022 to 2024, operating cash flow (before interest payments) was $250-$310M. Last year, it decreased to $165M as a result of their commodities not doing well.

The balance sheet has improved dramatically as the business has gone from ~$1.4B in debt 10 years ago to debt-free by the end of this year. As debt is fully paid off, management will likely transition to distributing all cash to shareholders. Even over the last few years, there have been times when the business was producing a lot of operating cash flow, which was mostly being absorbed by interest and principal payments on their debt. This is a significant change – so much so that I could argue that NRP is an entirely different business than it was before. The company is also a publicly traded partnership which prevents some investors from buying it.

With much less of the significant debt burden going forward, the company could produce $15-25 per share of free cash flow (pre-tax) in a more reasonable operating environment. There's not a perfect comp for NRP but other publicly traded partnerships trade between a 6-10% dividend yield. NRP should fall somewhere in the middle based on how its business stacks up. At $20 of cash flow and a 10% dividend yield, we could be looking at a $200 stock vs. $125 today. At an 8% yield, we're talking $250 or a double from here.

NRP is a high-quality business with a long reserve life that could potentially grow over time. Its commodities remain essential. There is also optionality in the company's large land base, which could be used for other productive purposes over time. For example, carbon capture initiatives are currently uneconomical, but NRP has participated in them in the past and could benefit if the economics improve.

The primary risks are a prolonged commodity downturn and poor capital allocation. While we are already in a commodity downturn which reduces some of this risk, conditions could worsen and lead producers to pause production. On capital allocation, management owns roughly 30% of the units, which gives me confidence they care deeply about how capital is deployed and have learned from past decisions.

To sum it all up, NRP is an inexpensive, quality business with some long-term optionality and a significantly improved balance sheet that should lead to a positive capital allocation change in the near future. It has a great risk reward and provides different exposures to the Right Tail Portfolio.

While the tools investors use will evolve, the principles that guide our decisions do not. Like that deck at Night Heron Park — built new, wrapped around something old — Right Tail's approach is evolving in form while staying rooted in what has always worked.

Best wishes for continued happiness and success,

Jeremy Kokemor
